actions.thought: Q4 offsuit is a very weak starting hand with poor connectivity and playability. In a three-handed game, while position on the button is valuable, this hand does not have enough equity to justify calling a full big blind or raising to steal. Folding is the most disciplined and highest EV decision to preserve my stack early in the tournament.

Tc5c is a very marginal hand, even blind versus blind. While it has some suited potential, playing out of position against the Big Blind with a 5-gapper is generally unprofitable. I prefer to fold and preserve my stack for better spots rather than bleeding chips with a weak holding.

With pocket 5s on the button in a 3-handed game, I have a strong enough hand to open the action. I'll make a standard raise to 2.5x the big blind to pressure the blinds, potentially steal the pot, or play a heads-up pot in position with the opportunity to set-mine.

I am in the Small Blind with 4h 3c, which is an extremely weak hand. The Button (Pro) has raised to 250. Calling here would put me out of position for the rest of the hand with a hand that has very poor equity and playability. Folding is the standard and most profitable play in this situation.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Eight suited, facing a raise from the button. The pot odds are favorable to defend, and my hand has good playability. A 3-bet is an option, but calling allows me to see a flop and control the pot size with a hand that has decent equity.

The board texture is highly coordinated with three diamonds, and I hold top pair with a weak kicker (Ace-Eight) but no diamond to protect against flushes. Betting into the pre-flop raiser here would likely only be called by hands that have me beat (better Aces or flushes) or would force out hands that I currently beat, which is inefficient. Checking allows me to control the pot size and re-evaluate based on the opponent's betting behavior, as top pair on this board is effectively a bluff-catcher.

The board is monotone with three diamonds (Td Ad 7d) and I hold an underpair with the 5 of diamonds. While having a diamond gives me some equity, it's a very low flush draw, and betting this wet texture into the big blind is risky since their range connects well with Aces, Tens, and higher flush draws. Checking behind allows me to control the pot size, realize my equity, and re-evaluate on the turn without facing a potential check-raise that would force me to fold.

The flop went check-check, indicating that neither player has a strong made hand or is willing to bet into the flush board. The turn is the Queen of Clubs, which doesn't complete the flush but adds potential straight draws. With top pair and an eight kicker, I still have a decent bluff catcher, but betting here is risky as it will likely only get called by hands that have me beat or fold out air. Checking is the most prudent play to control the pot size and navigate toward a cheap showdown.

With pocket 5s on a very wet and coordinated board (Td Ad 7d Qc), I have very little showdown value and only a marginal flush draw with the 5 of diamonds. The Big Blind has checked twice, which could indicate weakness or a trap with a made hand. Betting here as a bluff is risky because there are many better hands in the Big Blind's range that won't fold, such as any pair, higher diamond draws, or a straight draw. Checking behind again is the safest play to control the pot, realize my small amount of equity, and see a free river.

The board shows four diamonds (Td, Ad, 7d, 2d), creating a flush possibility for anyone holding a single diamond. My hand consists of a pair of Aces (with the 8 of hearts), meaning I have no diamond to compete with a flush. Since the betting has been passive (check-check) through the flop and turn, it is highly likely that neither player has a strong flush. However, betting here is polarized and risky; I am unlikely to be called by a worse hand, and any diamond in the opponent's range beats me. Therefore, checking is the optimal strategy to minimize risk and reach showdown with my pair of Aces.

The board has four diamonds (Td Ad 7d 2d). We hold the 5 of diamonds, giving us a very weak flush. Any opponent holding a 6 of diamonds or higher beats us. An opponent without a diamond will fold to a bet, while an opponent with a higher diamond will call or raise. Therefore, betting has no value, and checking behind is the best play to get to showdown safely.

Holding Ace-high on the button in a three-handed game is a standard opening situation. Raising here allows me to take control of the pot and potentially take down the blinds. A 2.5x raise is a standard size that provides a good risk-reward ratio.

King-Six offsuit is a weak hand to play from the small blind, especially against an opponent opening from the button. Being out of position makes it difficult to navigate post-flop play, and the risk of continuing with such a marginal holding outweighs any potential reward. Folding is the standard disciplined play to preserve my stack early in the tournament.

Holding King-Eight suited in the Big Blind against a Button open is a standard defense. The Button's opening range is typically wide, and K8s has decent playability and equity to see a flop. We are getting good pot odds to call the extra 150 chips. 3-betting could be an option as a bluff, but flat calling is the most standard, lower-variance line to realize our equity.

Holding middle pair with a strong kicker and a backdoor flush draw, checking to the pre-flop raiser is the standard play. I am out of position and want to control the pot size while keeping my opponent's bluffing range wide.

As the pre-flop aggressor with Ace-high on a relatively dry Queen-high board (Qh 6d 8s), a continuation bet is standard. This board texture favors my range as the Button, and a small bet of roughly 35-40% of the pot should be sufficient to force a fold from many of the Big Blind's missed hands. If called, I still have potential showdown value or can improve to top pair on an Ace.

We hold middle pair (Eights) with a strong King kicker, plus a backdoor flush draw. The opponent's continuation bet is relatively small (200 into 550), giving us excellent pot odds to continue. Folding middle pair to a single small c-bet against a wide button range is too weak, as we are often ahead of missed broadways, underpairs, or bluffs. Raising would unnecessarily overplay our hand and isolate us against strong hands like Qx or sets. Calling is the most balanced and standard play to realize our equity.

The turn gives us a King-high flush draw to go with our middle pair. We now have massive equity, but we are still out of position. The standard line is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. If he bets, we have an easy call (or potentially a check-raise depending on his sizing) given our strong draw and showdown value. Donking here isn't ideal as it allows stronger hands to call or raise while folding out bluffs, so we want to keep his bluffing range wide and control the pot.

The turn 2h is a brick that doesn't change the board texture significantly. After Pro called my flop continuation bet, their range likely includes marginal made hands like 8x, 6x, or pocket pairs, as well as some Queens. Checking back here allows me to control the pot size and take a free card to potentially hit an Ace on the river. It also protects my checking range and prevents me from being check-raised off my Ace-high equity.

The river pairs the six. My hand is now two pair, Eights and Sixes, with a King kicker. The opponent's turn check suggests marginal showdown value like Ace-high, medium pocket pairs (99-JJ), or missed draws. Betting for value is extremely thin here, as worse hands are unlikely to call, and better hands (like Qx or higher pocket pairs) will not fold. By checking, I can realize my showdown value against his giving-up range and potentially induce a bluff from busted straight or flush draws.

The river 6s pairs the board. Pro has checked to me for the third time (calling the flop and checking the turn and river). While my Ace-high has some showdown value, it is unlikely to win if Pro has any pair (which they likely do given the flop call). However, turning my hand into a bluff here is risky because the turn check-check line often leads to opponents calling down with marginal hands like middle pair. I will check back and take my chances at showdown, or concede the pot if beaten.

I am on the button with Queen-Three offsuit. This is a very weak starting hand that does not have the strength or connectivity to play profitably in a multi-way pot, especially considering it is the fourth hand and I have a healthy stack. Folding is the standard, disciplined play.

With QsJs in the small blind and the button folded, I have a very strong starting hand for a blind versus blind encounter. QsJs plays excellently post-flop and can hit strong top pairs, straights, and flushes. I will open-raise to 3 big blinds (300) to take the initiative, build the pot, and discourage the big blind from realizing their equity cheaply.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Deuce of spades. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300. I need to call 200 more into a pot that will be 600, giving me 3:1 pot odds. Suited Kings are strong enough to defend the Big Blind against a Small Blind open, especially since I will have position for the remainder of the hand. Folding would be too tight given the odds and my position.

On a Kh 5h Jc board, my QsJs gives me middle pair with a strong kicker. However, betting here often folds out worse hands while getting called by better ones like Kx, stronger Jx, or strong draws. By checking, I control the size of the pot, keep my opponent's range wide, and give myself the opportunity to evaluate their action, potentially check-calling to bluff-catch or see a turn card cheaply.

I have hit top pair on a board that contains both a flush draw and some straight draws. When Pro checks to me, I want to bet to extract value from weaker hands like a Jack or draws, and to protect my hand from giving a free card. While my kicker is weak, top pair is still very strong in a heads-up pot between the blinds. I will bet 300, which is half the pot, to charge any draws and see where I stand.

With QsJs on a Kh 5h Jc board, I have middle pair with a strong kicker. Flash bet half-pot (300) after my check. This bet size can represent a wide range, including top pairs, but also many draws (like heart flush draws, straight draws such as QT or T9) or complete bluffs. My hand is too strong to fold to a single bet, but raising would only get called by better hands and strong draws. Calling is the optimal line to keep his bluffs in and evaluate on the turn.

The turn card is the 7d, which is a relative blank that doesn't change the board texture significantly. I still hold middle pair (Jacks) with a Queen kicker. Leading out here (donk betting) would generally only get called by better hands (like Kx or AJ/KJ) and might fold out the bluffs or semi-bluffs that Flash was betting on the flop. By checking, I maintain pot control, allow Flash to continue bluffing with his missed draws, and can re-evaluate based on his turn sizing if he decides to bet again.

The 7d is a relative blank. I have top pair, but my kicker (Deuce) is the weakest possible. Pro has checked to me twice after calling my flop bet. While I could bet for value against a Jack or a draw, checking back provides pot control and allows me to reach the river safely. If I bet and get raised, I am likely beaten and forced to fold a hand with showdown value. By checking, I keep the pot manageable and can better evaluate the river based on Pro's next move.

The 4h on the river completes the obvious flush draw. Betting with middle pair here has no value, as worse hands will fold and better hands (like Kx, flushes, or better Jx) will call. Checking is the safest and most optimal play; it allows me to get to showdown cheaply against busted straight draws or safely fold if Flash bets strongly, representing the completed flush.

I have top pair with the weakest possible kicker (King-Deuce) on a board where a flush completed on the river (4h). Pro has checked to me for the third time this hand. While my hand is strong, my kicker is a major concern; any other King in Pro's range beats me. By checking back, I ensure I reach showdown and likely win against a Jack or a missed draw, while avoiding the risk of being check-raised by a better King or a flush. My hand has significant showdown value but is too thin to bet for value on this river.
Queen-Five offsuit is a very weak starting hand. From the Button in a 3-handed game, this hand falls well below the standard opening range. I will fold and save my chips for a more profitable spot.

I am in the Small Blind with K8o. After the Button folds, the action is on me. K8o is a strong enough hand to play aggressively against the Big Blind. I will raise to 300 (3x the big blind) to either take the pot down now or maintain initiative heading into the flop. Flashlight has shown some passivity in previous hands, so a raise is preferred over a call.

I am in the Big Blind defending against a raise from the Small Blind. With 7d 6s, I have a playable hand given the pot odds (getting 2:1 on the call) and the fact that I will have the positional advantage for the remainder of the hand as the Big Blind in heads-up play. Folding is too tight, and raising is not optimal with this holding, so calling is the most strategic move.

I have top pair with a King and an eight kicker on a very dry board (2s 2c Kd). Since the board is paired and uncoordinated, it is unlikely Flashlight has connected strongly unless they have a deuce or a better King. I will place a standard continuation bet of 200 (1/3 pot) to extract value from pocket pairs, weaker Kings, or Ace-high floats while keeping the pot manageable.

The flop of 2s 2c Kd is extremely dry and does not connect with my hand (7d 6s). I have no pair, no significant draw, and essentially zero showdown value against an opponent who has shown aggression pre-flop. While the pot odds of 4:1 (calling 200 to win 800) might seem appealing, continuing with 7-high on this board is likely to be a losing play, as I will have to fold on the vast majority of turns. Folding is the most disciplined action to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with Jack-Four offsuit. While this is a three-handed game where opening ranges are wider, J4o is still significantly below the threshold for a profitable opening hand. Both opponents have healthy stacks and have shown a willingness to play back or call. There is no strategic reason to commit chips with such a weak hand from this position.

With the button folding, I am left heads-up against the big blind. Holding 7-10 offsuit is a marginal hand, but given the 2:1 pot odds (calling 50 to win a pot of 150), it is profitable to complete the small blind and see a flop. Raising is too aggressive with this hand, and folding would be unnecessarily cautious.

With 8s 3c offsuit, I have a very weak hand. Since Flashlight limped in from the Small Blind, I can check my option in the Big Blind for free and see a flop. There is no justification to raise with such a poor hand, and folding makes no sense when a check costs nothing.

The flop of Ace-high is very unfavorable for my range, as an Ace is likely to have connected with the big blind's checking range. Holding 7-high with only backdoor straight possibilities, I have almost no equity here. Betting would be a pure bluff that is unlikely to succeed against a disciplined opponent on such a board. Therefore, checking is the standard play to minimize losses and re-evaluate on the turn.
With 8-high and no strong draws on an Ace-high board, there is no reason to invest chips into the pot as a bluff. Checking behind to see a free turn card is the standard and safest play.

With the turn card pairing the board and my hand containing only 7-high, I have minimal equity to compete against a checking range. Betting would likely be a futile bluff, so checking is the most standard, risk-averse play to control the pot size and re-evaluate on the river.

With 8-high and the board pairing the 5, I still have practically no equity. Flashlight checked again, giving me a free card to the river. There is no reason to risk any chips on a bluff in a limped pot with this weak of a holding. Checking is the optimal play.

With seven-high and no pair on an Ace-high board, my hand has no showdown value. Betting would be a bluff that is unlikely to force a fold from any pair, and checking is the most prudent way to conclude the hand without risking additional chips.

Flashlight checked the river. I have 8-high with no showdown value. However, the pot is small and it's a limped pot, so a bluff here might not be profitable enough to justify the risk. Flashlight could be trapping or have a weak pair they will call with. Checking behind is the safest play to conclude this hand without losing any chips.

6h 5d is a very weak starting hand with low equity in a three-handed game. Since I am the first to act pre-flop, calling a raise or simply calling the big blind with such marginal cards is not profitable long-term. Folding is the disciplined play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Holding 9-4 offsuit in the small blind is a very weak starting hand. With the button folding, I am facing the big blind with a hand that has extremely poor playability and equity. The optimal play is to simply fold and save my chips for a better opportunity.

We are on the button in a 3-handed game holding A7s. This is a very strong hand for this situation, well ahead of the blinds' average ranges. Raising to 2.5x BB (250) to steal the blinds or play a heads-up pot in position is optimal.

I am in the Small Blind with 7h 3s, which is an extremely weak hand. The Button (Pro) has opened for 250. There is no reason to defend this hand out of position, as it has poor connectivity and no high-card value. Folding is the standard and correct play.

7-4 offsuit is one of the weakest possible starting hands in Texas Hold'em. While the pot odds offer a relatively cheap call to see a flop, playing such a weak hand out of position against a button open is strategically poor. It is better to conserve my stack and wait for a more playable hand than to inflate the pot with a sub-optimal holding that will be difficult to navigate on the flop.

I am on the button with 9-6 offsuit. While 3-handed play allows for a wider opening range, 96o is near the bottom of my range and doesn't play well post-flop if called. I'll fold and look for a better spot.

With the button having folded, I am heads-up against the big blind. Queen-Jack offsuit is a strong enough hand to open with, and as the small blind, raising allows me to seize the initiative rather than simply limping in and playing defensively out of position post-flop. A raise to 300 is a standard sizing to put pressure on the big blind and potentially win the pot immediately or control the hand with the lead.

KQo is a strong starting hand, especially in a blind versus blind situation. Against a Small Blind open to 300, it easily warrants a defense. Calling keeps the opponent's opening range wide, allowing me to play in position post-flop and avoiding isolation against stronger hands like AK or AQ that might continue against a 3-bet.

The board [Td, Ad, 6h] is relatively dry, though it connects well with the big blind's calling range, which typically includes many Ace-x combinations. As the pre-flop aggressor, a continuation bet is the standard play to maintain initiative and potentially force a fold from weaker hands. My hand [Jd, Qh] offers a gutshot straight draw, giving me significant equity if I hit my King. A bet of 250, roughly 40% of the pot, effectively represents strength and allows me to semi-bluff out of position, putting pressure on my opponent while setting up further action if I improve.

On a flop of Td Ad 6h, my Qc Kh gives me a gutshot straight draw to the nuts (any Jack gives me Broadway). However, I only have 4 outs, which means I have roughly an 8.5% chance to hit my straight on the turn. Facing a c-bet of 250 into a pot of 600, my pot odds are roughly 22.7% (250 / 1100). The direct odds don't justify a call, and my King or Queen are likely not clean outs since my opponent's range heavily includes Ax hands that connect with this board. Folding is the mathematically sound play to preserve my stack.

My hand is 4c-8s, which is a very weak, unsuited combination with no connectivity or high-card value. Even with position on the button, there is no strategic justification for playing such a hand. The best move is to fold and wait for a more playable hand to maximize my stack's longevity.

I have 6h 9d offsuit in the Small Blind. This is a very weak hand, and even though the Button folded, it's not strong enough to limp or raise as a steal against the Big Blind. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here to conserve chips.
Q7o is a weak hand, especially in a 3-handed game. As the first to act pre-flop from the Button, folding is the standard and mathematically sound decision.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Eight offsuit (As 8d) and the Button has folded. This is a standard spot to raise into the Big Blind. A8o is a strong hand in a heads-up blind-versus-blind situation, as it likely dominates much of the Big Blind's calling range and has good high-card value. Raising to 300 (3x the BB) allows me to take control of the pot and potentially end the hand pre-flop or go to the flop with the initiative.

I am in the big blind with 6s 4s against a raise from the small blind. The pot odds are 2:1 (200 to call to win a 400 pot). While 6-4 suited is a marginal hand, it is within my defending range here given the price and position, allowing me to see a flop and potentially outplay the opponent if I connect.

In a blind-vs-blind battle, I raised pre-flop and was called by Flashlight. The flop of Jd-9d-5c is somewhat coordinated and hits a Big Blind defending range fairly well. However, as the pre-flop aggressor, I want to maintain the initiative. Ace-high still has decent equity, and a continuation bet can often take down the pot immediately if the opponent missed. If I am called, I will likely check-fold most turns unless I improve, but a 50% pot-sized bet here is a standard play to test the opponent's strength.

The flop of Jack-Nine-Five rainbow does not connect with my Six-Four of spades. With no pair, no flush draw, and no strong straight draw, I have zero equity against any reasonable betting range from the Small Blind. Calling a 300 chip bet into a 600 chip pot with total air is a negative expectation move that would waste chips early in the tournament. The most disciplined action is to fold and preserve my stack for a more favorable situation.

I am on the button with King-Jack offsuit (KsJc), which is a very strong starting hand in a three-handed game. I want to raise to build the pot and exert pressure on the blinds while playing in position. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate here.

Ten-eight offsuit is a marginal hand that performs poorly out of position. Calling the raise from the small blind puts me at a significant disadvantage for the rest of the hand against the button raiser. Given my stack size and the early stage of the tournament, it is more disciplined and higher EV to fold and wait for a better spot.

A9s is a strong starting hand. Against a Button raise from an active opponent, defending the Big Blind by calling is a standard and profitable play. It retains suited and high-card equity without bloating the pot out of position against a potentially strong range.

We defended the big blind with A9s. The flop comes K-J-4 rainbow (one heart), completely missing our hand except for an overcard and a backdoor flush draw. Out of position against the pre-flop raiser, the standard and safest play is to check to the aggressor. We will evaluate our next move based on whether they bet or check back.

I have flopped two pair (Kings and Jacks) on a relatively dry board. Pro checked to me, and I want to bet for value. My hand is very strong here, likely ahead of any single pair or drawing hand. A bet of around 60% of the pot (400) is a standard size to get calls from weaker Kings, Jacks, or straight draws like Queen-Ten.

Flash bet 400 into a 650 pot on a K-J-4 board. My A9s completely missed the flop, giving me only an overcard and weak backdoor draws. This board connects heavily with the pre-flop raiser's range, which includes many Kx, Jx, and broadway combinations. Being out of position with very little equity, folding is the most profitable and standard play.

King-Five offsuit is a marginal starting hand that performs poorly in multi-way pots. Given my position on the button, I prefer to fold and preserve my stack for a more high-equity opportunity rather than calling to see a flop with a dominated hand.

I am in the Small Blind facing only the Big Blind, but I hold an extremely weak and disconnected hand (84o). Despite only needing 50 chips to complete, 84o has terrible playability post-flop and poor equity. Playing this out of position is a long-term losing strategy. The optimal play is to simply fold.

I am holding 5h 2d offsuit on the button, which is one of the worst starting hands in Texas Hold'em. The mathematically correct play is to fold without hesitation and save my chips for better opportunities.

I am in the Small Blind with Q7o. The Button has folded, leaving the action between me and the Big Blind (Flashlight). Q7o is a marginal hand, but in a three-handed game, it is often strong enough to attempt a steal. Given my recent history of raising to 300 in late positions (Hands 5, 11, and 12), I will maintain consistency and raise to 300. This exerts pressure on the Big Blind and allows me to take down the pot pre-flop or play with the initiative post-flop.

I am in the Big Blind with 5d-8d. Facing a raise to 300 from the Small Blind (Flash) in a 400 pot, I have 2:1 pot odds. As a suited connector, 5d-8d has good playability post-flop, especially since I will have position throughout the hand against the Small Blind. Calling is the standard, profitable play here to see a flop and realize my equity.

I am the pre-flop aggressor in this hand. The flop of K-6-3 rainbow is very dry and favors the range of the pre-flop raiser, as I am more likely to have strong Kings (AK, KQ, KJ) or high pocket pairs in my range compared to the Big Blind who just called. This is a standard board to fire a continuation bet. I will bet 300 chips (50% of the pot) to exert pressure on Flashlight's range and try to win the pot immediately. If Flashlight raises, I can comfortably fold my Queen-high hand.

The opponent has made a half-pot continuation bet into a dry board. While my gutshot straight draw (needing a 4 or 7) has limited direct equity, I am in position, which allows me to control the pot size and potentially take it away on later streets if the opponent checks. Given the stack depths and the 3:1 pot odds, calling to see the turn is a standard play to realize my equity and leverage my positional advantage.

I am the pre-flop aggressor and fired a continuation bet on a dry King-high flop. Flashlight called my flop bet, which indicates they likely have a piece of the board, such as a King, a Six, or a pocket pair. The Turn (Ten of Clubs) doesn't significantly change the board texture or improve my hand (Queen-high with no draw). Since my opponent called on a very dry flop, they are unlikely to fold a King or even a middle pair to a second barrel. To avoid inflating the pot with a hand that has zero showdown value and no equity, I will check. If Flashlight bets, I intend to fold and conserve my stack.

I am in the Big Blind with 5d-8d. The turn board is 6d-Ks-3c-Tc. I have no pair and no legitimate straight or flush draw. The opponent (Small Blind) checked to me, which may indicate weakness, but betting into a King-high board when I have nothing is not a high-percentage bluff. Checking behind allows me to see the river for free and keep the pot small, avoiding bloating the pot with a hand that has no showdown value.

The board is 6d Ks 3c Tc 8h and I hold Queen-high (Q7o). I was the pre-flop aggressor and bet the flop, but was called. After checking the turn and seeing Flashlight check behind, it is likely they have a marginal made hand like a Six, an Eight, or a middle pocket pair that they are happy to take to a cheap showdown. My Queen-high has no showdown value, but bluffing into a range that has already shown interest by calling the flop is risky on such a dry board. I will check and hope to see a cheaper showdown or simply fold if faced with a bet, preserving my chip lead.

The opponent has checked to me on the river. With a pair of 8s, my hand has marginal showdown value. Betting is unlikely to generate value from worse hands, as the opponent would likely fold any air, and I would only be called by a better hand (like a King or Ten, or possibly a higher pair). Checking behind is the most prudent play to realize my showdown value and avoid inflating the pot with a weak hand.
JTo is a solid opening hand on the button in 3-handed play. I'll stick to my standard opening raise size of 300 to exert pressure on the blinds and build a pot with a hand that plays well post-flop.

78o is a weak, uncoordinated hand, especially playing out of position in the Small Blind against an aggressive open raise. Calling would place me in a disadvantageous spot post-flop with a range that struggles to connect well. Folding is the most disciplined choice to conserve my stack for better opportunities.

Holding 2c 3h in the Big Blind facing a 3x raise from the Button. This is an extremely weak, unsuited, and disconnected hand. The pot odds do not justify calling, and playing out of position with such a weak hand would be a massive mistake. The optimal play is to fold.

I hold a pocket pair of fours (4c, 4h) in the button position. Calling the 100 chips is a standard strategic move to 'set-mine,' meaning I want to see a cheap flop and hopefully hit a four to make trips. This allows me to control the pot size pre-flop while retaining a high potential for a strong hand if I connect on the board.

I hold 96o in the small blind, which is a very weak unsuited hand. Even though it's only 50 chips to call after the Button's limp, playing this garbage hand out of position against two players is unprofitable. Folding is the correct move.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-King offsuit, a premium starting hand. Flashlight limped from the button, and Pro folded. I should raise for value and to take initiative in the hand, as checking would allow Flashlight to see a flop for free with a likely wide and weaker range. A raise to 400 (4x the big blind) is a standard sizing to punish the limp and build the pot.

I hold a pocket pair of fours and am facing a raise from the big blind. With 100BB effective stacks, set-mining is a mathematically sound strategy. I have already committed 100, and calling the remaining 300 provides good implied odds to see a flop and attempt to crack a stronger hand. Folding would be too conservative, and 3-betting is likely overplaying the hand given the opponent's aggressive raise sizing.

I have flopped top pair with the best kicker (TPTK) on a board of Ac-9c-8s. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor and have a strong value hand, I should continue with a bet. This extracts value from weaker Aces, flush draws, and gutshot straight draws that Flashlight might have in their limp-call range. A bet of 450 into a pot of 850 is roughly half-pot, which is a standard sizing to build the pot while charging draws.

The flop of Ac 9c 8s is unfavorable for my pocket fours. My opponent's bet represents at least an Ace or a strong hand, and with no pair or draw, my equity is minimal. Calling here would be a mistake as I have no realistic chance to improve to the best hand in a meaningful way. Folding is the most profitable long-term decision to preserve my stack.

Holding a medium pocket pair (6c 6s) on the Button in a 3-handed game is a strong starting hand. Raising to 2.5x the big blind (250) is a standard open to try and steal the blinds or build a pot in position. If called, I can maneuver well post-flop, often looking to flop a set or use positional advantage to win the pot.

I have pocket deuces in the small blind. The button (Pro) opened for 2.5x. In a 3-handed game, pocket pairs have significant value even if they are the smallest. The stack depths (around 90BB) provide sufficient implied odds to set-mine. I will call the raise to see a flop, hoping to hit a set or navigate a low-texture board, while keeping the pot manageable out of position.

I have Ah-6h in the big blind. The pot offers great odds (150 to call into a 600 pot), and I am closing the action. A suited Ace is a strong enough hand to defend against an open and a call, as it has good post-flop potential.

On a board of 3s-3h-Td, my pocket deuces are currently an underpair to the board's high card (Ten) and are very vulnerable to any overcards or better pairs held by my two opponents. The board is paired with two treys, which significantly reduces the likelihood that any player holds a strong pair, although a Ten or a pocket pair is still possible. My Ace-high is essentially a weak bluff-catcher with minimal showdown value, so betting into two opponents on this texture is unlikely to generate value or fold equity. Checking is the most prudent play to control the pot size and re-evaluate on the turn.

With pocket sixes on a 3-3-10 board, my hand is somewhat vulnerable to overcards on the turn and river, but still likely the best hand if neither opponent has a ten or a better pocket pair. I have pocket deuces on a 3-3-T board. While I have a 'made hand' (two pair, 3s and 2s), it is incredibly weak in this 3-way pot. The board texture of 3-3-T is dry and relatively static, making it unlikely that the button opener has hit a strong hand, especially given the likelihood of a continuation bet. With Ace-high, I hold a hand with decent showdown potential against a wide range of bluffs or weaker high-card hands. The pot odds of 4:1 (250 to call into a 1000 pot) offer an excellent price to continue and see a turn card, as folding immediately would be too passive against a standard button continuation bet on a board that missed most pre-flop raising ranges.

The board has paired Threes and now includes a Ten and a Queen. I hold Ace-high (Ah-6h) with no pair and no significant draw, meaning my hand currently acts as a weak bluff-catcher. The opponent bet on the flop, suggesting they have at least a pair or a reasonable draw, and the Queen on the turn likely strengthens their range or hits a hand they were already betting. Betting into them would be a low-percentage bluff. Checking allows me to re-evaluate based on their next move while maintaining pot control.

On a turn board of 3-3-10-Q, my pocket sixes have now been counterfeit or outdrawn by any Queen, and I'm already behind any Ten or a slow-played Three. Since my opponent called my flop continuation bet, their range includes many hands that connect with this board, such as a Ten, diamond flush draws, or overcards that just paired the Queen. Betting here would likely only fold out worse hands and get called by better ones. Checking behind exercises pot control and allows me to evaluate the river while keeping my losses minimal if I am beaten.

The board is paired with Tens and Threes (Td-Ts-3s-3h), and the Queen (Qd) on the turn didn't improve my hand. My current best 5-card hand is Tens and Threes with an Ace kicker. Betting into my opponent is unlikely to extract value, as worse hands will fold and better hands (with a Queen) will call or raise. On the river, the board pairs the Ten, making the board 3-3-T-Q-T. My pocket sixes give me two pair, Tens and Sixes, with a Queen kicker. Betting here has no value because better hands will call and worse hands will fold. Checking behind is the only sensible option to realize my showdown value.

I have AKo on the button, which is a premium starting hand in a 3-handed game. I want to raise for value and to gain control of the pot. Raising to 3x the big blind is a standard opening size that helps define the hand and build a pot with my equity advantage.

Pocket eights are a strong hand in a 3-handed game, especially against a button open. By 3-betting to 950, I gain the initiative and put pressure on the button, allowing me to potentially take down the pot pre-flop or define the strength of the opponent's hand while holding a high-equity pair. I am dealt 3c 2h in the Big Blind, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Facing a raise from the Button and a 3-bet from the Small Blind, folding is the only viable option.

I have AKo, which is a premium hand in a 3-handed game. The opponent's 4-bet to 2300 in a 3-bet pot strongly indicates a premium hand range (likely QQ+ or AK). Committing more chips when I am likely behind or only set-mining is inefficient for tournament survival. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my remaining stack for a more advantageous situation.

Being on the button in a 3-handed game gives me a significant positional advantage. K7o is a weak, disconnected, offsuit hand. Facing a 3x open from the Button while in the Small Blind, it is an easy fold to avoid playing a marginal hand out of position.

5-6 offsuit is a very weak hand to defend from the Big Blind against a button open, even with decent pot odds. Being out of position makes it difficult to realize equity. I'll fold and wait for a better opportunity.

We are on the button with Q9o. This is generally too weak of an offsuit hand to open-raise with, as it has limited post-flop playability and is easily dominated. Folding is the most straightforward and solid option here.

I initially raised to 300 with King-Five offsuit from the Small Blind, but Flashlight in the Big Blind has responded with a significant 3-bet to 900. I hold K2 offsuit in the Small Blind facing a button raise from Flashlight. This is a very weak hand with reverse implied odds, easily dominated. Folding is the only correct play here.

I am on the button with Ace-Jack offsuit, which is a premium hand in 3-handed play. Standard strategy dictates an open-raise here to put pressure on the blinds and build a pot with a strong range. I'll raise to 300, which is consistent with my previous sizing and represents 3x the big blind.

I am in the small blind with suited connectors (6s 7s). While out of position, the hand has good playability post-flop and the pot odds for calling the 250 raise are reasonable. I will call to see the flop, keeping the pot manageable, and evaluate on the flop.

The board has paired the six and completed with a Queen, making the final texture 6s-4d-7h-6c-Qd. I have Ace-high with Ace-Jack. My opponent has checked to me on every street. Betting now offers no value as I will only be called by better hands, and there is a high likelihood my opponent is checking to trap or check-folding with worse high cards. Checking back allows me to take my Ace-high to showdown, which is the optimal way to realize my equity in this marginal spot.
Q6o is a weak offsuit hand, and not worth playing even from the Button. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

I am in the Small Blind with 8d 3h, which is a very weak starting hand. The Button has folded, leaving just me and the Big Blind. While the cost to complete the bet is only 50 more, 83 offsuit performs poorly post-flop and out of position. I will fold to avoid committing chips with a sub-optimal hand.

On the button in a 3-handed game, 87o is a reasonable hand to open for a steal, especially since I have a healthy stack. Raising to 300 has been my standard opening size, and it allows me to take control of the pot and use my positional advantage post-flop if called.

King-Deuce offsuit is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position. Facing a raise from the button in the small blind, the disciplined play is to fold and wait for a stronger starting hand or a better positional advantage. Defending with this hand would likely lead to difficult post-flop decisions.
I am in the Big Blind with Q4o facing a 3bb open from the Button. This hand is far too weak to defend out of position, so folding is the correct and standard play.

I am dealt a premium pocket pair, Pocket Jacks (Jh, Jc), on the button. This is a very strong hand in a three-handed game. I should look to be aggressive to either take down the pot immediately or narrow the field to go to the flop with a position advantage. A raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard open-raise size in this context, allowing me to build a pot while still maintaining control of the hand.
Holding 72 suited in the small blind against a button raise is a clear fold due to the hand's extremely low equity, poor playability, and positional disadvantage.

I am in the Big Blind with 7s 6c. Flashlight, on the button, has raised to 300. While the pot odds to call (200 into 450) are roughly 31%, 76o is a weak hand that plays poorly out of position. Given that I have a healthy stack and am close to the chip leader (Pro), I prefer to avoid marginal situations with mediocre offsuit connectors against a button raise.

In a 3-handed game, being on the button makes A6o a strong candidate for an open raise. By raising to 2.5x the big blind, I can often steal the blinds or take control of the hand in position if called.

I am in the Small Blind with 6c 5h. The Button (Pro) has raised to 2.5x the big blind. While 65o has some post-flop playability, calling out of position against a strong opponent with a mediocre hand is generally a losing play. Additionally, the Big Blind (Flashlight) is relatively short-stacked and might look to squeeze or defend aggressively. Folding is the most prudent move to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am in the Big Blind with a weak hand (4-5 offsuit). While the pot odds are tempting, this hand has poor playability and is dominated by the range of a button raiser. Calling is statistically negative EV, so folding is the correct strategic decision.

I am on the button with K3 suited, which is a strong enough hand to open in a three-handed game. Raising to 300 has been my standard opening size and allows me to take control of the pot early or potentially win the blinds and antes immediately.

Ace-Deuce offsuit is a weak hand to play out of position against an aggressive button open. The risks of being dominated by a better Ace or facing a difficult post-flop scenario outweigh the potential equity, so folding is the disciplined choice.

I am in the Big Blind with 7c 2s, which is statistically the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Facing a raise from the Button, there is absolutely no reason to play this hand. The correct and only play is to fold.

I am holding 7c-4h, which is an extremely weak hand with very little playability. As the shortest stack at the table (5400 chips), I need to play tighter and preserve my stack for better opportunities. There is no reason to invest chips in this hand, so folding is the clear strategic choice.

I am in the Small Blind with J2o. This is a very weak, disconnected offsuit hand. Even though the Button folded and I only have to complete the 50 chips, J2o is highly unprofitable to play out of position against the Big Blind. The standard and safest decision here is to fold.

I have A8 suited on the button. This is a strong starting hand in 3-handed play, easily in the top tier of hands to open. I will raise to 2.5x to steal the blinds or build a pot in position if called.

I am in the Small Blind with 6c 5d, which is a very weak hand. Pro has raised to 250 from the Button. While the pot odds are roughly 3:1, playing such a marginal hand out of position for the rest of the hand is generally negative expected value. Given my healthy stack size and the poor quality of my hole cards, folding is the most disciplined and standard play here.

I am in the big blind with pocket sevens (7h, 7s). The Button (Pro) has raised to 250. The Small Blind (Flash) has folded. I need to call 150 more to see a flop. Pocket pairs are strong hands that play well heads-up. With 4:1 pot odds, calling is a mathematically sound decision. Raising is an option, but calling allows me to keep the pot manageable and play post-flop while retaining the potential to flop a set.

I am in the big blind and hold pocket sevens on a flop of Qd 8c 6h. This is a middling board that favors the pre-flop aggressor (the Button). I have a pair of sevens and a gutshot straight draw, giving me some equity, but I am out of position. The most strategic play is to check to the aggressor to control the pot size and see how they proceed, rather than betting into them.

I flopped middle pair with the top kicker and a backdoor nut flush draw. While betting for protection and thin value is an option, checking behind is an excellent play for pot control. It keeps my opponent's range wide, allows me to catch bluffs on later streets, and keeps the pot manageable with a medium-strength hand.

The board has paired the sixes (6d on turn). My hand of pocket sevens now improves to two pair (Sevens and Sixes with a Queen kicker). However, this hand remains a marginal bluff-catcher as any Queen or any pocket pair higher than 7s (like 8s, 9s, 10s, JJs, QQs, KKs, AAs) beats me. Pro checked the flop, indicating a lack of a strong Queen or a missed continuation bet. Checking the turn allows me to control the pot size while seeing if Pro decides to bet or check behind. If Pro checks again, I get a free look at the river.

With middle pair top kicker and the nut flush draw on a paired board, I have a very strong hand. Flashlight has checked twice, indicating weakness or a marginal made hand. Betting here serves multiple purposes: it extracts value from worse hands like smaller pairs or draws, denies free equity to overcards, and builds the pot in case I hit my flush on the river.

I am in the Big Blind with pocket sevens on a board of Qd-8c-6h-6d. My hand has improved to two pair (Sevens and Sixes). Pro, on the button, checked the flop but is now betting 300 on the turn. Pro's check on the flop is a significant indicator of weakness, suggesting they likely do not have a strong Queen, as that would usually be a standard continuation bet. Their turn bet is likely a probe or a marginal value bet. Given the pot odds and the potential for a bluff from Pro, calling is the most rational decision to keep the pot manageable and evaluate the river.

I am in the Big Blind with pocket sevens (7h, 7s) on a river board of Qd-8c-6h-6d-Ts. My hand is two pair (sevens and sixes), which effectively functions as a bluff-catcher. Given that any Queen, ten, eight, or six beats my two pair, and the opponent (Pro) showed strength by betting on the turn, I am unlikely to get called by worse hands if I bet. Checking is the most strategic action to preserve my chips and potentially reach showdown if the opponent checks back, or to fold to a significant bet if the opponent decides to value-bet a better hand.

The river brings a Ten, which improves some of Flashlight's floating hands like JTs or T9s that might have called the turn. My eights and sixes are largely a bluff-catcher at this point, as very few worse hands will call a value bet here. Checking behind ensures we get to showdown, realizing our equity against busted draws and weaker pairs, while avoiding value-owning ourselves against a Ten or a trapped Queen.

I am on the button with J9o in a 3-handed game. This is a standard opening hand to put pressure on the blinds. My stack is healthy, and a 3x open (300 chips) has been my consistent sizing for button opens throughout the tournament. This allows me to take control of the pot and utilize position post-flop if called.

I am in the small blind with suited connectors 9d 5d. While out of position, the price to call (250 into a pot of 450, potentially more if the big blind calls) offers decent implied odds to see a flop. Flash is playing aggressively, but I have enough stack to see a flop and re-evaluate.

K4o is a very weak offsuit hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially multiway and out of position. Facing a 3x raise from the button and a call from the small blind, folding is the standard and most profitable decision.

The flop of Ts-7s-8d gives me a gutshot straight draw (needing a 6 to complete a 6-7-8-9-T straight). I am out of position against an aggressive button player (Flash). Checking is the standard play here to see how Flash acts on this coordinated board. If Flash bets, I will evaluate the sizing, but for now, checking is the most cautious and strategic move to maintain control over the pot size.

I have an open-ended straight draw (J-10-9-8-7) with the 9c and Js on a Ts-7s-8d board. This is a very strong semi-bluffing candidate. By betting now, I can potentially win the pot immediately if Flashlight has a weak hand that missed the board, or I can build a larger pot for when I complete my straight on the turn or river. I am in position, which allows me to dictate the pace of the hand. A bet of 400 (slightly over half-pot) is a standard continuation bet on a wet board like this.

I have a gutshot straight draw with 9d-5d on a board of Ts-7s-8d. While I have a gutshot, the pot odds are poor (400 to call in a 1100 pot, requiring about 26.6% equity, whereas my direct equity is only about 8.5% with 4 outs). Being out of position against an aggressive player like Flash makes it difficult to realize equity if I don't hit on the turn. Folding is the most disciplined and mathematically sound decision here to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with a pocket pair of sixes, which is a strong hand in a 3-handed game. My stack is significantly smaller than my opponents', so I need to play aggressively to accumulate chips. Raising to 300 is the standard opening size to put pressure on the blinds and potentially take down the pot pre-flop or gain initiative with a playable hand.

ATo is ahead of a typical Button open range. By 3-betting from the Small Blind, I can fold out the Big Blind, gain the betting initiative, and often win the pot pre-flop against Flashlight's wide range.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Four suited. The button (Flashlight) opened for 3x, and the Small Blind (Pro) 3-bet to 900. While suited Kings have some playability, Kd 4d is a weak starting hand to defend against a 3-bet, especially when out of position against Pro, who has shown significant aggression. Calling 800 more into a 1300 pot with a hand that is likely dominated by Pro's 3-betting range (which usually includes AK, KQ, and high pairs) is a losing play in the long run. Folding preserves my stack for better opportunities.

I am facing a 3-bet from the small blind (Pro) after my initial raise on the button with pocket sixes. Pocket sixes are a strong hand, but they perform best when they hit a set. Given my stack size of 4250 (42.5 BBs), calling the 600 to see a flop is a prudent choice. I have position on the Pro, which will allow me to better navigate post-flop play. If I hit a six, I have a very strong hand to stack off with. If the board comes overcards, I can easily fold to a continuation bet, minimizing my losses while maintaining enough stack to play the next hands.

I have top pair with a ten kicker, but the board is monotone diamonds and I hold no diamond. This is a very dangerous texture, especially in a 3-bet pot. Checking controls the size of the pot, protects my stack, and prevents me from getting blown off my equity by a raise. It also allows my opponent to potentially bluff or check back weaker hands.

The flop of Ad 7d 3d is highly coordinated with a flush draw and an Ace, which is dangerous for my pocket sixes. With no diamond in my hand, my pair is weak against an opponent who 3-bet pre-flop and is now checking. Betting here is risky as I would likely only be called by better hands or raised, forcing me to fold. Checking back is the most prudent play to control the pot size and see a turn card cheaply.

The turn gives me top two pair (Aces and Tens). Since the flop checked through, it's less likely Flashlight has a made flush, though he could certainly hold a single high diamond. Betting here is mandatory for both value and protection; I want to charge any single diamond draws and get value from weaker Aces or pairs that decided to check the flop. A bet size of about half pot sets up a comfortable river shove given his remaining stack.

The board is extremely dangerous (Ad 7d 3d Th). My pocket sixes are behind almost any range that 3-bet pre-flop and is now betting into me on the turn. There is a flush draw present, an Ace, and a Ten; my hand has no diamonds or any other redraws. Calling 900 into a pot of 2700 is poor value as I am likely drawing dead or close to it. Folding is the disciplined play to conserve my remaining 3650 chips for a more favorable situation.
I am on the Button with a very weak, disconnected suited hand (6d 2d). Even though I have position, this hand is too weak to play profitably. Folding is the standard and correct decision.

I am in the Small Blind with a strong hand (KQo) after the Button has folded. Raising is the standard play here to put pressure on the Big Blind, especially given that Flashlight is relatively short-stacked with about 35.5 big blinds. A 3x raise to 300 chips is a standard opening size in this spot.

My hand, Jack-six offsuit, is quite weak and unsuitable for defending the big blind against an aggressive small blind raise. Given my current stack size of 3550, it is more prudent to fold and preserve my chips for a more favorable situation.

I am on the button with 7s 4h, which is an extremely weak hand. While I have been aggressive from the button in previous hands, 74o is near the bottom of my range and does not justify a raise or a call, especially with a relatively short stack in the small blind (Flashlight) and a chip leader in the big blind (Pro) who have both shown willingness to defend. Folding is the most profitable long-term play here.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Ten offsuit after the Button folded. This is a strong hand for heads-up play. Raising is the optimal strategy to take initiative and put pressure on the Big Blind, rather than just completing the blind and allowing a free realization of equity.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Seven offsuit, which is a weak hand. The Small Blind (Flashlight), who is playing a short stack, has open-raised to 3x the big blind. This suggests they have a relatively strong hand and are willing to commit chips. J7o has very poor equity against a reasonable opening range and is easily dominated. The most profitable play is to fold and preserve my stack.

Pocket nines are a strong hand, especially on the button in a short-handed game. I need to play aggressively to build my stack, as I am currently short-stacked relative to my opponents. Raising is the standard play to either pick up the blinds or play a heads-up pot in position.

Holding an unsuited and semi-connected weak hand like 8d 6c in the small blind, playing out of position against a button raise is unprofitable. It's a standard fold to avoid committing chips with a marginal hand.

I am in the Big Blind holding 3d 4d, a low suited connector. Flashlight (Button) has opened to 300, and Pro (Small Blind) has folded. I need to call 200 more into a pot of 450, giving me 2.25:1 pot odds. While 3d 4d is a very speculative hand, its ability to hit straights and flushes makes it a viable defending hand in the Big Blind against a late-position raise. My stack is very healthy compared to Flashlight's remaining chips, allowing me to take this small risk to see a flop and potentially crack a stronger starting hand if I hit the board hard.

On a flop of 7s-8c-5d, I have an open-ended straight draw with 3d-4d. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop raiser (Flashlight), the standard play is to check to the aggressor. My hand has significant potential, and I want to see how Flashlight reacts to the board before committing more chips. If they bet, I have the correct odds to continue with an eight-out draw.

I have an overpair (99) on a coordinated board (7-8-5). While the board is dangerous with many straight draws possible, I currently hold the best hand against most of the opponent's range. Betting allows me to protect my equity against overcards and gain value from draws or weaker pairs (like an 8 or 7). A bet of 350 is appropriate to size up against the pot of 650.

On a flop of 7s-8c-5d, I have an open-ended straight draw (any 6 or 2). Flashlight has bet 350 into a pot of 650. The pot odds are approximately 2.85:1, which is sufficient to call with an eight-out draw (roughly 31.5% equity by the river). Given Flashlight's relatively short stack (3000), I have good implied odds if I hit my straight on the turn or river. Raising as a semi-bluff is an option, but calling allows me to see the turn card more cheaply and keep Flashlight's range wide, including potential bluffs that I can beat if I improve.

On the turn, the Ace of Diamonds is a card that significantly favors the pre-flop raiser's range (Flashlight). My hand, 3d 4d, has picked up a flush draw to go along with the open-ended straight draw, giving me roughly 15 outs (9 diamonds + 6 non-diamond 6s and 2s). Since I am out of position and Flashlight was the aggressor on the flop, the most prudent play is to check. This allows me to see how Flashlight reacts to the Ace. If they check behind, I get a free card to hit my massive draw. If they bet, I have more than enough equity to call and potentially realize my implied odds against their remaining stack.

The turn card (Ace of Diamonds) is a significant scare card for my pocket nines. My opponent called my flop bet, which suggests they have at least a pair or a strong draw. The Ace is much more likely to have helped their range (as calling pre-flop with Ace-high hands is standard). Betting here would likely only be called by better hands (any Ace or two-pair), forcing me to play a bloated pot with the second-best hand. Checking back is the optimal play to pot-control, keep the game situation stable, and potentially see a showdown cheaply or re-evaluate on the river.

On the river, the board is 7s-8c-5d-Ad-Jc. I have 4-high (3d 4d), having missed both my open-ended straight draw and the flush draw I picked up on the turn. Flashlight (the pre-flop aggressor) checked behind on the turn when the Ace appeared, which suggests they likely do not have an Ace. Their range is capped at middle pairs (99, TT), or a piece of the board like a 7 or an 8. While I could attempt a bluff to represent the Ace or the Jack, Flashlight's relatively short stack (3,000 chips remaining) makes them more likely to call down with any pair to avoid being bullied. Given that I have zero showdown value, I will check. If Flashlight checks back, I lose the pot as expected. If they bet, I will fold, as my hand has no potential to win a showdown and I have a significant stack lead overall.

The board runout (7s-8c-5d-Ad-Jc) is very unfavorable for my pocket nines, as it connects heavily with the typical calling ranges that include broadway cards like the Ace and Jack. My opponent check-called on the flop but checked the turn and river. While I have showdown value, it is extremely thin given the overcards on board. Betting here would likely only be called by a better hand, while checking allows me to realize my showdown value or mitigate losses if I am beat. Checking back is the standard play here.
J7o is a very weak starting hand, even from the button. Folding is the best choice to avoid getting involved in a marginal situation with a hand that has poor playability post-flop.

I am in the Small Blind with Q2o. The Button (Pro) has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind (Flashlight). Flashlight is short-stacked with only 4250 chips. While Q2o is a weak hand, I only need to put in 50 more chips to see a flop. Given the pot odds and the fact that we are head-to-head, calling to see a flop is a reasonable low-risk play, though folding is also acceptable. I will choose to call the 50 to see if I can hit the board.

I am in the big blind with a very weak hand (3c 4s). The small blind has completed the bet, and since I am already in the big blind, I have the option to check and see the flop for free. There is no strategic value in raising with this holding, so checking is the optimal play.

I have Queen-high on a very connected board (8-9-10). This board likely hits the Big Blind's range well, and I have no real equity or draws here. I will check and hope to see a free turn card, but I am prepared to fold to most bets.

I am in the big blind with a very weak hand (3c 4s) on a connected board of 8-T-9. The small blind has checked. There is absolutely no reason to bet here with no equity, so checking behind to see a free turn card is the only logical decision.

I have improved to top pair with my Queen on the turn. However, the board is extremely coordinated with 8-9-10-Q, meaning any Jack now has a straight. Given the connected nature of the board and my weak kicker (Deuce), my hand is marginal. I will check to control the pot size and see how the Big Blind responds, as they could easily have a straight, two pair, or a draw that they might semi-bluff.

I am in the big blind with a very weak hand (3c 4s) on a highly connected board (8-T-9-Q). I have no pair and no real draw. Since my opponent checked to me, there is no reason to attempt a bluff in this small pot. Checking behind is the most conservative and strategic play to see the river for free.

I have top pair on a very connected board (8-9-10-Q-3). While top pair is usually a strong hand, any Jack completes a straight, and my Deuce kicker is extremely weak. The hand has been checked through every street, suggesting Flashlight likely has a marginal hand or a weak draw. By checking the river, I can reach a showdown for free and likely win against middle pairs (9s or 10s) or missed draws, without risking a raise from a straight or a better Queen.

I hold a pair of threes (3c with the 3h on the board), which has very little showdown value against a range that might contain any higher pair or better kicker. Since my opponent has checked through every street including the river, they are likely either trapping or, more probably, folding air. There is no value in betting as I will rarely get called by a worse hand, so checking behind is the optimal way to reach showdown safely.

I am on the button with 62o, which is a very weak hand. Even in a 3-handed game, this hand is not strong enough to open or call with, especially with two active opponents in the blinds. I will fold to preserve my stack and wait for a better spot.

The button has folded, leaving me heads-up against the big blind. With Jack-Five offsuit, I have sufficient pot odds to complete the small blind. Calling 50 chips to see a flop in this heads-up situation is a standard, low-risk play.

I have Pocket Aces (Ah As) in the Big Blind. The Small Blind has just limped in. I need to raise for maximum value and to build the pot. A raise to 350 is a solid sizing to punish the limp, extract value from worse hands, and potentially induce a limp-reraise if the Small Blind is trapping.

I am in the small blind with Jack-Five offsuit. After my initial call of the 50 chip small blind, the big blind raised to 350. Jack-Five offsuit is a very weak hand that lacks sufficient equity to defend against a raise from the big blind, especially out of position. Calling 300 chips more to see a flop with such a marginal hand is a negative expected value play. The most disciplined strategy is to fold and preserve my stack for a better spot.

I am on the button with Ace-King offsuit, which is a premium starting hand in a 3-handed game. Given my stack size and position, a standard open raise of 3x the big blind (300) is the optimal play to take control of the hand, potentially isolate an opponent, or build a pot with my strong equity.

Facing a 3x raise from the Button, 9s6s is a suited gapper that is too weak to play from the Small Blind. Calling would put us out of position with a marginal hand, and 3-betting is an unnecessary risk given the hand's weak equity and the Big Blind still left to act. Folding is the standard and most profitable play.

I have Ace-Four of diamonds in the Big Blind. This is a very strong starting hand in a three-handed game, especially suited. Flashlight, the button, has raised to 300. Flashlight has the smallest stack at the table (3850). While I could 3-bet to put pressure on them, calling is also a very viable option to keep the pot manageable and see a flop with a hand that plays well post-flop. However, given my stack size and the strength of Ad4d, a raise is effective for isolating or taking the pot down immediately. Since Flashlight is relatively short-stacked, I will raise to 900 to put them in a difficult spot and potentially isolate.

I opened with a standard raise on the button with Ace-King offsuit, a premium hand. Flash (Big Blind) responded with a 3-bet to 900. Given my stack size of 3850, I am not committed to shoving yet, as that would isolate me against better hands or force out weaker ones I dominate. Calling the 600 is the most balanced approach, keeping the SPR manageable and allowing me to realize my equity post-flop, especially since the opponent's 3-bet range from the big blind can be quite wide.

I was the pre-flop aggressor with a 3-bet. Although the flop (3c-7c-6h) is low and doesn't directly hit my Ace-Four of diamonds, I still maintain a range advantage as the 3-bettor. A continuation bet of 650 (approximately 35% of the pot) allows me to maintain initiative and potentially fold out broadway hands like KQ or JT that missed this board entirely. If Flashlight shows resistance by calling or raising, I will have to re-evaluate on the turn, but for now, the standard play is to continue the aggression.

The opponent's bet of 650 into a 2500 pot is relatively small, giving me excellent pot odds to continue. With Ace-King offsuit, I hold two overcards, and the Ace of clubs provides a backdoor flush draw, offering sufficient equity to call. I am not yet committed to a large pot, but folding is too conservative given the board texture and the relative strength of my hand against a potentially wide continuation-betting range.

The Ten of Clubs is a poor card for my range and my specific hand (Ad4d). It completes the club flush and provides several straight possibilities (though less likely given the pre-flop 3-bet). Flashlight has called a 3-bet and a flop c-bet, suggesting they have a piece of this board or a draw. With only 2600 chips remaining in their stack and a pot of 3150, I have no fold equity against any pair or draw. Checking is the best option to see if Flashlight checks back, giving me a free river, or to fold if they commit the rest of their stack, as I currently have no made hand or significant draw.

The opponent checked the turn after I called the flop, which often indicates weakness or a willingness to control the pot size. With the Ace of clubs in my hand, I hold the nut flush draw on this board containing three clubs. Checking behind gives me a free card, but betting allows me to capitalize on my significant equity, apply pressure, and potentially take down the pot immediately if the opponent is on a missed draw or a weaker pair. A bet of 1200 into the 3150 pot provides enough pressure to force folds from marginal hands while setting up a potential river shove should the club complete or if I decide to bluff.

On the turn, the board has become increasingly coordinated with the Ten of Clubs, completing possible flushes and straights (98, 54). Flashlight has called a pre-flop 3-bet and a flop continuation bet, indicating they have a piece of the board. My Ace-high (Ad4d) has very little equity against their range, which likely includes pairs, flushes, or straights. Flashlight's bet of 1200 into a 3150 pot, leaving them with only 1400 behind, suggests they are committed to the hand. I do not have the pot odds to call for a non-existent draw or to hope my Ace-high is currently the best hand. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack.

With pocket twos on the button, I have a clear opening raise. It plays well if I can steal the blinds, and if I get called, I am set-mining in position with a stack depth that allows for good implied odds against both players.

In a 3-handed game, Ace-high hands have significant value against a wide Button opening range. While Ace-Four offsuit is a marginal hand to play out of position, it is generally too strong to fold to a standard 2.5x raise. 3-betting is an option to take the initiative, but calling allows me to see a flop and potentially win a small-to-medium pot if I connect or if the board is checked down, while also limiting my losses if Pro has a dominating hand.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-high offsuit (Jc 8h), which is a marginal hand that plays poorly in a multi-way pot. With the button opening and the small blind calling, I am facing a multi-way scenario out of position. Given the strength of the hand and the lack of initiative, calling is not profitable. Folding is the most disciplined choice to preserve my stack for a better spot.

I have flopped top pair with an Ace, though my kicker is weak (4). On a dry board like As-Tc-5d, it is standard to check to the pre-flop aggressor (Pro) to see how they value their hand. Donk betting here might scare away hands I can beat or get me raised by better Aces. By checking, I can maintain pot control and likely call a continuation bet to evaluate the turn.

On this A-T-5 rainbow board, my pocket twos have very little equity and essentially no backdoor potential. While the board favors my pre-flop raising range, 22 is one of the worst hands in my range to use as a continuation bet bluff because it has no progression on future streets. I will check back to realize my 2-outer equity for free and look to fold if faced with aggression on later streets.

I have top pair with a weak kicker. On the flop, both I and the pre-flop aggressor (Pro) checked on a dry Ace-high board. This check from Pro often indicates a medium-strength hand or a complete miss, as an Ace would typically continuation bet for value. The Nine of Hearts on the turn doesn't change much. I will check again to maintain pot control and induce bluffs from weaker hands or to see a cheap showdown. I have AhQh on the button, which is a premium starting hand in a 3-handed game. I will raise to 300 to build the pot and take the initiative, which is consistent with my standard opening size in previous hands.

9-7 offsuit is a weak starting hand with very poor playability, especially out of position in the small blind against an aggressive button open. Calling would be a negative expected value (-EV) play, so the disciplined choice is to fold and wait for a better spot.

Holding A4s in the Big Blind facing a Button open, defending is a standard and profitable play. Suited Aces play well post-flop by flopping nut flush draws, wheel draws, and occasional top pairs. Calling keeps the pot manageable out of position while realizing the equity of this hand.

Out of position as the pre-flop caller, it is standard to check to the pre-flop aggressor. Although I flopped top pair with top kicker and a wheel gutshot, checking allows Flash to c-bet his bluffs and overcards, which I can then respond to appropriately.

I have two overcards (Ace, Queen) and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 5) on a low, connected board. With top pair (Ace kicker) and a gutshot to a wheel straight on a low 2-3-4 rainbow board, my hand is very strong against a button opening range. By check-calling, I keep his c-bet bluffs in the pot, control the pot size out of position, and can comfortably proceed to the turn to realize my equity.

On the turn, an 8 of clubs improves my equity slightly by giving me a backdoor flush draw to go with my top pair and wheel gutshot. However, I am still out of position and my top pair is only a pair of fours, which can be easily beaten by overpairs or made straights like A5 or 56. The standard play here is to check to the pre-flop aggressor, maintaining pot control while letting him fire another barrel with bluffs or check back his weaker made hands.

The turn card (8c) is essentially a brick that doesn't change the board texture significantly. Having been called on the flop, Pro likely has a small pair or a draw. My Ace-high still has decent equity with two overcards and a gutshot straight draw. On the river, my pair of Fours with an Ace kicker has marginal showdown value. I have Ace-high with a Queen kicker. The board ended up fairly disconnected, with no straights or flushes possible. Q8o is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position. I am in the Big Blind with Q7o. Flashlight limped from the button and the Small Blind folded. Q7o is a mediocre hand, but since it's a limped pot and I am already in for the big blind, checking is the most standard play to see a flop for free without bloating the pot out of position.

I am first to act on a very dry flop of T-2-6. I have Queen-high with no draws and very little equity. In a limped pot, there is no need to bloat the pot with a weak hand while out of position. I have flopped a set of deuces on a relatively dry board (Th-2s-6d). This is an incredibly strong hand. With my opponent checking, I need to start building the pot to get value. I am in the Big Blind with Q7o in a limped pot. The flop is T-2-6 rainbow, which is very dry. I checked to Flashlight, who has now bet 150 into a 250 pot (total pot now 400). With 54 suited on the Button, I have a decent drawing hand that plays well post-flop, and I can often steal the blinds right here. Given my deep stack and positional advantage, an open-raise to my standard 2.5x (250) applies pressure to the blinds and allows me to take control of the hand.

I am in the Small Blind with a small pocket pair (3s 3c). Pro has raised to 2.5x from the button, which is a standard opening size. I am in the big blind, getting 4-to-1 pot odds (150 to call a 600-chip pot). Although 8h-3h is a weak hand, the price to call is excellent and I am closing the action pre-flop, allowing me to see a flop with good pot equity.

I have flopped a set of threes on a board of Jack-Five-Three with two diamonds. This is a very strong hand. I have bottom pair on a Jc-5d-3d board. Being out of position against two opponents with a relatively weak hand, checking is the optimal strategy to control the pot size and observe my opponents' intentions without bloating the pot unnecessarily.

With middle pair and a weak kicker on a fairly wet flop (two diamonds), I have some showdown value but my hand isn't strong enough to bet for value against two opponents. Checking behind allows me to control the pot size, realize my equity, and keep weaker hands and bluffs in my opponents' ranges.

I have a set of threes (3s 3c on a board of Jc 5d 3d Tc). After the flop went check-check-check, I missed an opportunity to build the pot. With a pair of fives and a club flush draw picked up on the turn, I have a strong combo draw with plenty of outs (9 clubs, plus potential outs to improve to trips or two pair). I have a set of threes, which is a very strong hand. On the river, the board brings a King, making my pair of fives incredibly weak (5th pair). KTo is a marginal offsuit hand, and playing it from the Small Blind against a Button raise puts us out of position post-flop with a hand easily dominated by stronger Kings or Aces. Folding is the most profitable and standard play here.

I am the pre-flop aggressor after 3-betting from the Big Blind.
